seo.title: CQC Compliance and Staffing | Vigil Care Staffing
seo.description: How care providers align staffing, evidence and supervision to the CQC single assessment framework and Regulation 18 this quarter.

nap.address: Ferguson House, 113 Cranbrook Road, Ilford IG1 4PU

fields.sub: How care providers align staffing, evidence and supervision to the CQC single assessment framework and Regulation 18.
fields.heading: CQC Compliance and Staffing: What's Changing This Quarter
fields.image_alt: Care manager reviewing staffing and compliance records

fields.body: CQC compliance in staffing is about being able to show, with evidence, that you have enough suitably skilled people on shift to meet residents' needs safely. Regulation 18 of the Health and Social Care Act regulations sets the expectation for sufficient numbers of suitably qualified, competent and experienced staff. The single assessment framework then asks providers to evidence this through quality statements on an ongoing basis, rather than through one-off inspections.
fields.heading: The essentials

fields.body: The direction of travel is towards continuous, evidence-led assurance. Inspectors increasingly expect to see planned staffing compared against actual staffing, clear supervision records, and up-to-date competency evidence for every worker, including agency staff. Providers who keep this evidence current throughout the year, rather than assembling it before an inspection, are far better placed. Building a light-touch quarterly refresh into your routine keeps records inspection-ready at all times.
fields.heading: What's changing this quarter, at a glance

items.body: Keep a record of planned rotas against the hours actually worked, with reasons for any variance and how gaps were covered.
items.title: Planned versus actual staffing

items.body: Confirm every worker, permanent or agency, has valid right-to-work evidence and an appropriate DBS check on file.
items.title: Right-to-work and DBS

items.body: Check that safeguarding, infection prevention, moving and handling and medication training are current for all staff on shift.
items.title: Mandatory training and competencies

items.body: Maintain a supervision cadence for each worker, with dated notes that show the issues raised and the actions taken.
items.title: Supervision and appraisal

items.body: Hold compliance profiles for agency staff so you can evidence their vetting and training alongside your own team.
items.title: Agency worker assurance

items.body: Assign clear ownership for staffing compliance, so someone is accountable for keeping evidence complete and current.
items.title: Compliance and assurance

items.body: Agree how rota, training and supervision records are stored and updated, so evidence is easy to retrieve at short notice.
items.title: Documentation and information flow

items.body: Track a small set of measures, such as shift-fill rates and training-compliance percentages, to spot risks early.
items.title: KPIs and workforce quality assurance

items.body: Set a predictable schedule for supervisions and observations so development and oversight happen consistently.
items.title: Supervision cadence model

items.body: Review and update your staffing evidence each quarter so nothing lapses between formal assessments.
items.title: Quarterly evidence refresh

items.a: It requires providers to deploy sufficient numbers of suitably qualified, competent, skilled and experienced staff to meet people's needs safely at all times.
items.q: What does Regulation 18 require for staffing?

items.a: It asks providers to evidence safe staffing through quality statements on an ongoing basis, rather than relying on a single inspection snapshot.
items.q: How does the single assessment framework affect staffing evidence?

items.a: Keep a compliance profile for each agency worker covering DBS, right-to-work, references and mandatory training. A vetted staffing partner can supply this to support your records.
items.q: How should we evidence agency staff compliance?
